result:
The rain doesn't fall here the way it does in other places. In this city it arrives sideways, almost embarrassed, like it knows it's interrupting something important. People still walk through it with the same determined expressions they wear on clear days, as if getting wet is a personal failing rather than meteorology.

I keep thinking about how strange it is that we've built our entire civilization on the assumption that tomorrow will be mostly like today. We plant crops, sign thirty-year mortgages, fall in love—all these acts of outrageous optimism that the patterns we've observed will continue. The universe doesn't owe us continuity. It doesn't owe us anything.

There's a particular shade of blue that appears in the sky right after a storm that I haven't seen anywhere else. It's not the cheerful robin's-egg blue of children's drawings or the deep naval blue of twilight. This is something wounded and ancient, like the universe is showing you its bruises for just a moment before covering them again with ordinary daylight.

I wonder what the first person to ever feel nostalgia felt. Was there a moment in some prehistoric cave when someone suddenly missed a yesterday that had seemed utterly ordinary at the time? Did they have language for that ache yet, or did they just sit with this new, impossible feeling that something was gone forever?

The coffee is getting cold and the neighbor's dog is barking at nothing again. Life continues its peculiar habit of being simultaneously mundane and miraculous. I suppose that's what makes it worth writing about at all. (249 words)
